elements.sent-2411-001: It has long been believed that the atomic dynamics in disordered structures, such as undercooled liquids and pre-melted interfaces, are characterized by collective atomic rearrangements in the form of quasi-one-dimensional chains of atomic displacements (strings) and their closed forms (rings).
elements.sent-2411-002: Here, we show by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations that strings do not form by a single collective event.
elements.sent-2411-003: Instead, they are simply MD trajectories of propagating local density perturbations, which we call densitons.
elements.sent-2411-004: The atoms on this trajectory are physically indistinguishable from their environments except for the moving head of the string (densiton).
elements.sent-2411-005: A densiton migrates by either single-atom jumps or a concerted rearrangement of 2-3 atoms.
elements.sent-2411-006: The simulations reveal a remarkable similarity between the strings in disordered and crystalline structures, in which the densitons localize into point defects.
elements.sent-2411-007: This work calls for a significant reinterpretation of the string concept and instead proposes a densiton model of the atomic dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-008: The atomic dynamics in fully or partially disordered materials controls many of their properties, such as diffusive mass transport, viscosity, and heat conduction \citep{derlet2021viscosity,Derlet:2020aa,Derlet:2018aa,AlSm1,Berthier2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Despite decades of dedicated research efforts, the atomic dynamics of disordered systems remains one of the least understood phenomena in materials physics.
elements.sent-2411-010: One of the prominent features of such systems is the dynamic heterogeneity in space and time \citep{Donati_1999,Douglas98,Kob_97,AlSm1,chandler2010dynamics,jung2005dynamical,10.1063/1.1644539,Karmakar:2009aa,Cui:2001aa .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Namely, the atomic mobility is distributed highly non-uniformly across the system, and this distribution constantly changes over time.
elements.sent-2411-012: The dynamic heterogeneity has been investigated by several experimental probes \citep{Marcus:1999aa,Cui:2001aa .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the most detailed information about the underlying atomic mechanisms comes from computer simulations.
elements.sent-2411-014: In particular, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations have revealed that atomic displacements often occur in the form of chains, or strings, encompassing a group of atoms whose displacements are such that one atom jumps into the previous location of another \citep{Donati_1999,Douglas98,10.1063/1.1644539,Kob_97 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: That other atom, in turn, jumps into the previous position of yet another atom, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-016: Occasionally, a string can be closed to itself, forming what is called a ring.
elements.sent-2411-017: Strings and rings were observed in amorphous solids and undercooled glass-forming liquids \citep{Donati_1999,Douglas98,Kob_97,AlSm1,chandler2010dynamics,jung2005dynamical,10.1063/1.1644539 , polymers \citep{AlSm1 , super-ionic solids \citep{annamareddy_superionic , disordered grain boundaries \citep{Warren2009,Zhang2006,Chesser:2022aa,Mishin:2015ac , phase boundaries \citep{Al-Si-IPB,Chesser:2024aa , dislocation cores \citep{Chesser:2024aa , and open surfaces \citep{Zhang2010,Salez:2015aa .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: It is generally believed that a string is a quasi-one-dimensional dynamic object representing a collective (also called cooperative or correlated) displacement of a group of atoms \citep{Erhart2020,10.1063/1.1644539,Donati_1999,Douglas98,Kob_97,AlSm1 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In MD simulations, the strings are revealed by the following procedure \citep{Douglas98 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: First, mobile atoms are identified by the criterion that their displacements $\Delta r$ during a chosen time interval $\Delta t$ lie within the range $0.4r_{0 <\Delta r<1.2r_{0}$, where $r_{0}$ is an average interatomic distance in the system.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Here, the lower bound eliminates immobile atoms and the upper bound eliminates multiple atomic hops.
elements.sent-2411-022: The second criterion selects pairs $(i,j)$ of mobile atoms that remain nearest neighbors of each other during the interval $\Delta t$: $\text{min (|\mathbf{r _{i (t)-\mathbf{r _{j (0)|,|\mathbf{r _{j (t)-\mathbf{r _{i (0)|)<0.43r_{0}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Mobile pairs satisfying this criterion and sharing an atom are then joined together into a mobile cluster, which often looks like a string or a ring.
elements.sent-2411-024: In most cases, $\Delta t$ is set as the position $t^{*}$ of the maximum of the non-Gaussian parameter $\mathrm{NGP =3\left\langle (\Delta r)^{4}\right\rangle /5\left\langle (\Delta r)^{2}\right\rangle ^{2 -1$ as a function of time.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Alternatively, $\Delta t$ is identified with the maximum of the average string length computed as a function of time, which is usually close to $t^{*}$ \citep{10.1063/1.1644539 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Yet another method identifies $\Delta t$ with the inflection point on the mean-square displacement (MSD) plot as a function of time also giving a similar value.
elements.sent-2411-027: Typical string length identified by the above criteria varies from $n=2$ to $3$ atoms at high temperatures to $n=10$ or more atoms at low temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-028: As an example, Fig.~\ref{fig:1 demonstrates a 10-atom string in a disordered grain boundary in copper.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this case, $\Delta t$ is set to the maximum position $t^{* =17$ ps on the NGP curve.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Statistical properties of strings and rings in diverse materials have been studied in great detail \citep{Donati_1999,Douglas98,Kob_97,AlSm1,chandler2010dynamics,jung2005dynamical,10.1063/1.1644539 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, their origin and the formation and growth mechanisms remain poorly understood.
elements.sent-2411-032: The MD simulations reported in this article reveal that strings containing more than 2-3 atoms do not form by a single coordinated/cooperative atomic displacement.
elements.sent-2411-033: Instead, such strings represent computer-generated trajectories of density fluctuations propagating through the material by a series of discrete atomic rearrangements.
elements.sent-2411-034: The length of such trajectories carries valuable information about the relaxation dynamics of the density fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-035: However, the present simulations challenge the previously claimed association of the strings with collective displacements of large ($n\gtrsim3$) atomic groups.
elements.sent-2411-036: That association significantly overestimates the degree of coordination among atomic movements in disordered systems.
elements.sent-2411-037: To set the stage for the analysis of atomic dynamics in disordered structures, we start by examining the atomic trajectories during self-diffusion in crystalline solids mediated by vacancies and interstitial atoms.
elements.sent-2411-038: Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (a) illustrates typical chains of atomic displacements caused by these defects in crystalline Cu.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Note that these chains look very similar to the strings widely observed in structurally disordered systems.
elements.sent-2411-040: The images are snapshots of the strings at sequential points in time, showing their growth direction.
elements.sent-2411-041: Each string has a head (the last atom joining the string in the growth direction) and a tail (the first atom that initiated the string).
elements.sent-2411-042: If the string is created by a vacancy, the individual atomic displacements are opposite to the growth direction.
elements.sent-2411-043: If the string is created by an interstitial, the atomic displacements are in the growth direction.
elements.sent-2411-044: We call these growth mechanisms backward propagation and forward propagation, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-045: It is important to note that the vacancy and the interstitial execute an uncorrelated random walk by a Markov process.
elements.sent-2411-046: After each defect jump, the system has plenty of time to thermalize (de-phase) the atomic trajectories, erasing all memory of all previous jumps.
elements.sent-2411-047: This process is typically described in terms of the transition-state theory \citep{Vineyard:1957vo .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Thus, the strings shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (a) represent nothing more than computer-generated atomic trajectories caused by the point defect migration.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The atoms forming the strings are physically indistinguishable from the surrounding atoms, except for the head atom, which possess excess energy due to its proximity to the defect.
elements.sent-2411-050: This is evident in Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (a), where the atoms are color-coded by the potential energy.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In other words, the only physically distinct part of a string is its moving head.
elements.sent-2411-052: The rest of the string is a ghost object that only exists due to the computer's ability to label and track individual atoms.
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that a point defect is associated with a positive (interstitial) or negative (vacancy) local deviation of the atomic density from the background value.
elements.sent-2411-054: We refer to both types of the local excess density as a ``densiton'', which can be either positive (e.g., interstitial) or negative (e.g., vacancy).
elements.sent-2411-055: While this term is redundant in the context of crystalline solids, it facilitates the subsequent discussion of strings in disordered systems, in which vacancies and interstitials are not well-defined while the respective density fluctuations play similar roles.
elements.sent-2411-056: In this terminology, we can say that the strings shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (a) are trajectories of the positive and negative densitons.}
elements.sent-2411-057: A moving positive densiton causes a forward propagation of atomic displacements, while a moving negative densiton causes a backward propagation.
elements.sent-2411-058: Each displacement of a negative densiton is accomplished by an atomic jump in the opposite direction, filling the low-density site and creating a new low-density site (densiton) behind.
elements.sent-2411-059: This is the mechanism of the backward propagation.
elements.sent-2411-060: A displacement of a positive densiton can be a more complex process.
elements.sent-2411-061: At relatively low temperatures, interstitial atoms in Cu form a split dumbbell configuration parallel to $\left\langle 100\right\rangle $ directions.
elements.sent-2411-062: A dumbbell jumps by a concerted displacement of three atoms moving the dumbbell center into a nearest-neighboring lattice site with a simultaneous $90^{\circ}$ rotation.
elements.sent-2411-063: Thus, the forward propagation caused by a positive densiton occurs by a chain of atomic rearrangements involving a collective displacement of three atoms.
elements.sent-2411-064: At high temperatures, however, thermal fluctuations nearly destroy the close coordination of the three atoms, and the process is better described by a chain of individual atomic jumps.
elements.sent-2411-065: It is important to recognize that the forward propagation of a string is only possible due to the indirect interstitial (also called interstitialcy) mechanism, in which each displacement of a positive densiton moves a new group of atoms.
elements.sent-2411-066: As a result, each atom in a long string only undergoes a nearest-neighbor displacement.
elements.sent-2411-067: The direct interstitial mechanism, in which a single interstitial atom hops between interstitial positions, does not produce a string.
elements.sent-2411-068: This is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (a) for a Ta interstitial atom in Cu.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Instead of a string, the MD simulation produces a single long arrow pointing to the time-dependent position of the Ta atom.
elements.sent-2411-070: The three atomic mechanisms discussed here are summarized in the conceptual diagram in Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-071: Finally, note that the strings in crystalline systems can grow to a very long but finite size.
elements.sent-2411-072: Several mechanisms can stop the growth.
elements.sent-2411-073: A winding trajectory can accidentally cross itself, breaking the string into two parts.
elements.sent-2411-074: Furthermore, if two trajectories accidentally cross, each of them will be chopped into two parts.
elements.sent-2411-075: In a relatively small system, the maximum string length is on the order of the system dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-076: If the crystal contains both vacancies and interstitials, they can mutually annihilate, terminating two strings.
elements.sent-2411-077: To summarize the above analysis, the standard algorithm based on the $\Delta t=t^{*}$ time interval identifies relatively long strings comprising $n>3$ atoms.
elements.sent-2411-078: Such strings do not form by a single collective event.
elements.sent-2411-079: They represent a chain of dynamically uncorrelated atomic rearrangements involving from 1 to 3 atoms.
elements.sent-2411-080: A string grows by the motion of a densiton located at the string's head.
elements.sent-2411-081: The string is simply the densiton's trajectory, which is physically indistinguishable from the background except for its head.
elements.sent-2411-082: Depending on the densiton's sign, the growth occurs by either forward or backward propagation of discrete atomic displacements.
elements.sent-2411-083: The string growth continues as long as the densiton exists.
elements.sent-2411-084: The observation of a moving positive densiton indicates that the atomic displacements propagate by an indirect mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-085: We next consider strings in structurally disordered systems.
elements.sent-2411-086: We have studied the formation and growth of strings and rings in undercooled liquids and thermally disordered interfaces such as grain boundaries and interphase boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-087: The materials that we studied included elemental Cu and Si, as well as Al-Si interfaces obtained by simulated vapor deposition and Cu-Ta interfaces obtained by the direct bonding method.
elements.sent-2411-088: Most of the results are presented in the Supplementary Information file.
elements.sent-2411-089: Representative examples are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Disordered_1 for strings in a Cu grain boundary and in undercooled Cu liquid.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Note the striking similarity with the strings produced by vacancy and interstitial migration in crystalline Cu (cf.~Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-091: In both ordered and disordered systems, some strings grow by forward propagation of atomic jumps while others grow by backward propagation.
elements.sent-2411-092: In all cases studied here, the atoms forming a string could not be physically distinguished from the surrounding atoms in the MD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-093: The concept of a vacancy in disordered structures is not well-defined due to the absence of an underlying lattice.
elements.sent-2411-094: However, the observation of back-propagating strings suggests the existence of similar local-low-density structures that can migrate by a vacancy-like mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-095: Namely, one of the nearby atoms jumps into the low-density ``cage'' and fills it, leaving a similar low-density spot behind.
elements.sent-2411-096: Repeated atomic rearrangements of this type create a chain of atomic displacements in the direction opposite to the low-density propagation (Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-097: Recognizing that such vacancy-like low-density structures are not ``real'' vacancies, we unify both crystalline and disordered cases under the term of a negative densiton.
elements.sent-2411-098: Similarly, in the absence of a crystalline lattice, interstitials in disordered structures are not well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-099: However, the existence of forward-propagating strings is strong evidence that disordered structures can host similar local-high-density excitations, which we call positive densitons.
elements.sent-2411-100: They can migrate through the system by causing a chain of relatively short ($\approx r_{0}$) atomic displacements in the forward direction, creating a forward-propagating string.
elements.sent-2411-101: This mechanism is similar to the indirect interstitial migration mechanism in crystalline solids.
elements.sent-2411-102: Other authors also suggested the existence of a high concentration of interstitial-like ``defects'' in liquid phases with properties similar to those of real interstitials existing in the respective solid phase near the melting point \citep{Nordlund2005,Ashkenazy2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-103: The link between strings and densitons is further demonstrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:Disordered_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: In one test, a few atoms were randomly inserted into a disordered grain boundary, creating a set of high-density spots.
elements.sent-2411-105: In another test, a few atoms were randomly removed from the same grain boundary, creating a set of low-density spots.
elements.sent-2411-106: In other words, we have artificially planted a set of positive or negative densitons into the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-107: In the subsequent MD simulations, most of the positive densitons drifted away from the initial location and created a forward propagating string.
elements.sent-2411-108: Likewise, most of the negative densitons gave rise to a backward-propagating string.
elements.sent-2411-109: These tests demonstrate that densitons can persist for an extended period of time and migrate by creating strings.
elements.sent-2411-110: In some cases, a negative densiton would not produce a string and would, instead, dissolve in the surrounding structure (Fig.~\ref{fig:Disordered_2 (c)).}
elements.sent-2411-111: Such cases provide examples of short-lived densitons whose lifetime is shorter than the inverse frequency of atomic jumps during the string growth.
elements.sent-2411-112: Similar scenarios were earlier observed by Delaye and Limoge \citep{Limoge-1993 , who examined static structural relaxations in a Lennard-Jones glass after removing an atom.}
elements.sent-2411-113: They found that the displacements of neighboring atoms could be large enough to fill the void and restore the average atomic density.
elements.sent-2411-114: In other cases, the displacements could be small enough to preserve the local excess free volume.
elements.sent-2411-115: In yet another scenario, the relaxation had the form of a ``cooperative evolution'' involving many atoms.
elements.sent-2411-116: The latter process apparently represented a string nucleation, but its nature was not studied in detail.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Exploring the timescales of string evolution
elements.sent-2411-117: The string growth involves several different time scales.
elements.sent-2411-118: We will discuss them starting with a crystalline system.
elements.sent-2411-119: Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice-MD displays several log-log plots of the MSD versus time $\Delta t$ over several orders of magnitude.}
elements.sent-2411-120: For the defect-free Cu lattice, the plot is linear with a slope of two in the short time limit.
elements.sent-2411-121: This linear behavior represents the ballistic motion of the atoms.
elements.sent-2411-122: At $\Delta t\approx10^{-1}$ ps, the plot reaches a local maximum followed by a few oscillations, signaling a dynamic transition from the ballistics to atomic vibrations.
elements.sent-2411-123: The time interval of this transition is approximately $10^{-1}\lesssim\Delta t\lesssim1$ ps, which is comparable to the inverse of the Debye frequency of Cu ($\nu_{D =7.14\times10^{12}$ Hz at 0 K).}
elements.sent-2411-124: This transition is followed by a horizontal segment representing the MSD of atomic vibrations at 1000 K.
elements.sent-2411-125: For systems with a single point defect, the MSD curve shows an upward deviation from the perfect-lattice behavior when $\Delta t$ reaches the timescale of string nucleation (i.e., activation of point-defect jumps) (Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice-MD (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-126: This time is shorter for an interstitial than for a vacancy due to the lower activation barrier.
elements.sent-2411-127: The horizontal segment on the curve evolves into a shoulder.
elements.sent-2411-128: Since point-defect jumps are memoryless, the jump activation time is the same as the average increment of string growth, which we call the jump waiting time $\tau_{w}$.
elements.sent-2411-129: To quantify this time for vacancy-induced strings, we computed MSD curves for three vacancy concentrations.
elements.sent-2411-130: As expected, the vacancy-induced deviation of the MSD curve from the lattice behavior starts earlier when the vacancy concentration increases (Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice-MD (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-131: Note, however, this shift of the curves does not mean that the string activation time $\tau_{w}$ becomes shorter.
elements.sent-2411-132: Since the vacancies do not interact with each other, $\tau_{w}$ does not change.
elements.sent-2411-133: This example demonstrates that the location of the shoulder on the MSD curves does not characterize the string activation time.
elements.sent-2411-134: To demonstrate this point further, we have computed the average number $N_{vj}$ of vacancy jumps in the system as a function of time for the three vacancy concentrations.
elements.sent-2411-135: While $N_{vj}$ increases with the number $N_{v}$ of vacancies present in the system, the number of jumps per vacancy, $N_{vj /N_{v}$, does not change.}
elements.sent-2411-136: In other words, the $N_{vj /N_{v}$ versus time plots for different $N_{v}$ collapse into a single master curve representing the true dynamics of a single vacancy (Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice-MD (c)).}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Using this master curve, we can estimate $\tau_{w}$ as the time for which $N_{vj /N_{v =1$, which is a few picoseconds.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Returning to (Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice-MD (b)), note that the NGP maximum occurs on a significantly longer timescale than $\tau_{w}$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: On this timescale, a vacancy can make over 10 jumps.
elements.sent-2411-140: Moreover, the NGP peak position shifts with the vacancy concentration.
elements.sent-2411-141: Given the previously discussed similarity between the ordered and disordered system, the following conclusions can be made: (1) the time $t^{*}$ commonly used for string identification is too long ($\gg\tau_{w}$) and overlooks the key information about the string growth, and (2) $t^{*}$ reflects not only the growth of individual strings but also the degree of disorder in the system.
elements.sent-2411-142: In our terminology, $t^{*}$ depends not only on the densiton dynamics but also on their concentration.
elements.sent-2411-143: Disentangling the two factors is not an easy task.
elements.sent-2411-144: Another relevant timescale is one on which the atomic rearrangements occur at the head of the string.
elements.sent-2411-145: The rearrangement/transition time, which we denote $\tau_{t}$, is significantly shorter than the waiting time $\tau_{w}$.
elements.sent-2411-146: For lattice strings, $\tau_{t}$ is roughly on the order of $1/\nu_{D}$.
elements.sent-2411-147: For concerted atomic rearrangements involving several atoms, $\tau_{t}$ is longer but still below $\tau_{w}$.
elements.sent-2411-148: The diagram in the top right corner of Fig.~\ref{fig:Strings-rings explains the difference between $\tau_{w}$ and $\tau_{t}$ using a plot of atomic displacements versus time for a set of atoms involved in a string.}
elements.sent-2411-149: This graphical format \citep{10.1063/1.1644539,annamareddy_superionic,Erhart2020 allows one to distinguish between sequential atomic jumps and truly collective events.}
elements.sent-2411-150: In the latter case, several curves bunch together into a single transition.
elements.sent-2411-151: Fig.~\ref{fig:Strings-rings summarizes such plots for both sequential and concerted atomic rearrangements in ordered and disordered systems, highlighting the differences between $\tau_{w}$ and $\tau_{t}$.}
elements.sent-2411-152: In addition to open strings, Fig.~\ref{fig:Strings-rings shows rings, which are observed less frequently.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Like open strings, rings can also evolve by either a sequence of single-atom jumps or as a single concerted displacement of an atomic group.
elements.sent-2411-154: The bottom right corner in Fig.~\ref{fig:Strings-rings shows a rare case of a five-atom ring in a grain boundary, which forms by a single concerted atomic displacement.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Previous MD simulations of disordered systems were focused on collective statistical metrics characterizing the atomic dynamics on diverse length and time scales.
elements.sent-2411-156: In the only investigation of atomic displacements in individual strings known to us \citep{10.1063/1.1644539 , the displacements were found to propagate sequentially with occasional collective jumps of small atomic groups called ``micro-strings''.}
elements.sent-2411-157: However, each string was treated as a physically district dynamical object with a ``backbone'' hosting the micro-strings.
elements.sent-2411-158: Furthermore, the previous studies identified the strings using a timescale $\Delta t\approx t^{*}$ on which the strings had grown relatively long and were unlikely to have formed by a single concerted rearrangement of the participating atoms.
elements.sent-2411-159: However, because an entire string emerged in the simulation during the time increment $\Delta t$, this gave the impression that the atoms had moved collectively.
elements.sent-2411-160: In the present study, we have focused on a detailed analysis of individual atomic trajectories with small ($\ll\tau_{t}$) time increments.
elements.sent-2411-161: We find that strings containing more than a few atoms do not form by a single collective rearrangement.
elements.sent-2411-162: Instead, they grow by a sequence of dynamically uncorrelated increments.
elements.sent-2411-163: Each increment occurs by a local atomic rearrangement at the head of the string.
elements.sent-2411-164: The rearrangement involves either a single-atom jump or a concerted displacement of 2-3 atoms.
elements.sent-2411-165: In either case, the local rearrangement happens very fast (on the $\tau_{t}$ timescale) while the waiting time $\tau_{w}$ between sequential rearrangements is significantly longer.
elements.sent-2411-166: Importantly, the part of the string left behind the moving head is fully relaxed and physically indistinguishable from the surrounding atoms.
elements.sent-2411-167: It can only be revealed by tracking the atoms in MD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-168: Only the moving head of the string is physically distinct due to its excess energy and (positive or negative) local free volume.
elements.sent-2411-169: The simulations have revealed a remarkable similarity between the strings and rings in disordered structures and the atomic trajectories created by vacancy and interstitial migration in crystalline solids.
elements.sent-2411-170: To reflect this similarity, we describe the string growth in both ordered and disordered structures as migration of positive (interstitial-like) or negative (vacancy-like) densitons.
elements.sent-2411-171: The densiton sign can be determined in MD simulations from the direction of the atomic displacements relative to the direction of the densiton migration (i.e., the direction of string growth).
elements.sent-2411-172: We have shown that positive densitons migrate by forward propagation while negative densitons migrate by backward propagation.
elements.sent-2411-173: The strings created by moving densitons in disordered structures are analogs to the MD trajectories of point defects in crystals.
elements.sent-2411-174: Densitons have a distribution of lifetimes.
elements.sent-2411-175: Short-lived densitons delocalize before they can migrate.
elements.sent-2411-176: Densitons with a longer life (persistent densitons) migrate and can create relatively long strings before they delocalize or encounter another densiton of the opposite sign.
elements.sent-2411-177: Interpretation of the average string length $\overline{l}$ is not straightforward because it involves several factors.
elements.sent-2411-178: On one hand, $\overline{l}$ depends on the densitons' lifetime, which is a physical factor.
elements.sent-2411-179: On the other hand, MD simulations capture purely computational string-truncation processes such as their intersections and self-intersections.
elements.sent-2411-180: The present simulations call for a significant reinterpretation of the strings and rings in disordered structures as simply MD trajectories of moving densitons and not physically distinct dynamic objects formed by a collective atomic rearrangement.
elements.sent-2411-181: Rather than keeping track of the strings, the atomic dynamics can be better described by a random walk of positive and negative densitons populating the system and exhibiting a spectrum of mobilities and lifetimes.
elements.sent-2411-182: Fluctuations in the densiton concentration cause the dynamic heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-183: The densiton migration can be responsible for the atomic self-diffusion and possibly other transport properties.
elements.sent-2411-185: The MD simulations were performed using the GPU-accelerated implementation of the Large-scale Atomic/Molecular Massively Parallel Simulator (LAMMPS)~\citep{Plimpton95 .}
elements.sent-2411-186: Visualization and analysis of atomic configurations utilized the Open Visualization Tool (OVITO)~\citep{Stukowski2010a .}
elements.sent-2411-187: The MD integration time step was set to 0.1 fs.
elements.sent-2411-188: Atomic interactions were described using the embedded atom method interatomic potential for Cu \citep{Mishin01 , the modified Tersoff potential for Si~\citep{Purja-Pun:2017aa , the angular embedded atom potential for Al\textendash Si~\citep{Saidi_2014 , and angular-dependent interatomic potential for the Cu\textendash Ta system~\citep{G.P.-Purja-Pun:2015aa .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: The single-crystalline Cu simulations used a fully periodic cubic supercell whose size varied between 3.6 nm and 10.8 nm, depending on the simulation goal.
elements.sent-2411-190: Vacancies were created by randomly removing atoms to achieve the target vacancy concentration.
elements.sent-2411-191: To study interstitial defects, a single Cu or Ta interstitial atom was introduced.
elements.sent-2411-192: Multiple interstitials were not created to avoid their clustering.
elements.sent-2411-193: The Si simulations utilized a $5.4$ nm fully periodic cubic supercell.
elements.sent-2411-194: A single vacancy or a single interstitial atom were introduced into the system, creating a point-defect concentration of $1.25\times10^{-4}$.
elements.sent-2411-195: The amorphous Si model was constructed in a $10.9$ nm supercell following the protocol described in Ref.~\citep{Moon:2021aa .}
elements.sent-2411-196: A Cu bicrystal with a $\Sigma29\{250\}$ tilt grain boundary was generated using the $\gamma$-surface method~\citep{Mishin:1998aa .}
elements.sent-2411-197: The bicrystal had the approximate dimensions of $15\times15\times30$ nm.
elements.sent-2411-198: Periodic boundary conditions were applied in lateral directions parallel to the boundary plane, while free surfaces were imposed along the normal direction.
elements.sent-2411-199: To preserve the periodicity of the grain boundary structure, the system dimensions were slightly adjusted to ensure an integer number of unit cells within the simulation box.
elements.sent-2411-200: A Si bicrystal containing a $\Sigma85\{100\}$ twist grain boundary had similar boundary conditions and was also generated using the $\gamma$-surface method~\citep{Mishin:1998aa .}
elements.sent-2411-202: The atomic displacements were studied in the NVT ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-203: An Al(110)/Si(001) interphase boundary was constructed by a vapor deposition simulation.
elements.sent-2411-204: A layer of Al$_{0.905}$(Si$_{0.095}$) solution was deposited onto a Si(001) substrate from a gas phase at the temperature of 648 K.
elements.sent-2411-206: Details of the vapor deposition can be found in Ref.~\citep{li2024atomistic .}
elements.sent-2411-207: The layer composition and temperature were chosen so as to keep the alloy within the single-phase region on the left of the solvus line on the Al-Si phase diagram \citep{Al-Si-IPB computed with the interatomic potential.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Diffusion processes were investigated in the NVT ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-209: A Cu(110)/Ta(110) interphase boundary was created by bonding Cu and Ta single crystals with the desired crystallographic orientations.
elements.sent-2411-210: The system was equilibrated by semi-grand canonical Monte Carlo simulations using the parallel Monte Carlo code ParaGrandMC \citep{ParaGrandMC .}
elements.sent-2411-211: Careful consideration was given to minimizing the lattice mismatch between the two bonded materials.
elements.sent-2411-212: The cross-sectional area of the interface was $16.2\times17.8$ nm$^{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-213: Periodic boundary conditions were applied in the in-plane directions with surface boundary conditions normal to the interface.
elements.sent-2411-214: The system was equilibrated by isothermal anneals at various temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-215: Interface diffusion was studied by NVE MD simulations with various durations up to 20~ns.
elements.sent-2411-216: The Debye frequency of Cu $\nu_{D}$ was estimated from $\nu_{D =k_{B T_{D /h$ using the Debye temperature of $T_{D =343$ K \citep{Kittel .. Data availability.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: The data supporting the findings of this study are available in the Supplementary Information file or from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
elements.sent-2411-218: The computer simulation part of this research used the publicly available codes LAMMPS and OVITO.
elements.sent-2411-219: The routine computer scripts controlling the execution of the calculations are not central to this work but are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

elements.sent-2411-224: Y.~M.~contributed to the concept development, supervised the effort, and prepared the first draft of the complete manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-225: All co-authors participated in the manuscript editing and approved its final version.

elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{Figure1} \caption{The traditional method of string identification in MD simulations. (a) A bicrystal of copper with a $\Sigma29$ $(250)$ tilt grain boundary at 1000 K. The snapshot visualizes only mobile atoms with displacements larger than $1.6r_{0}$ during a time interval of $\Delta t=t^{*}$. The atoms are colored according to their location, with yellow and red representing the grain boundary and surface atoms, respectively. (b) Snapshot of a typical string formed at the grain boundary. The gray arrows represent atomic displacements during the time $\Delta t$. The string was identified by the methods traditionally applied to glass-forming fluids. (c) Mean square displacement $\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle $ and the non-Gaussian parameter (NGP) of the system as a function of $\Delta t$. The time $t^{*}=17$ ps corresponds to the maximum of the NGP. \label{fig:1}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{Figure2} \par\end{centering} \caption{Strings in crystalline solids. (a) String-like trajectories of atomic displacements in the copper lattice at 1000 K caused by vacancy diffusion and interstitial diffusion by the indirect mechanism. For comparison, the bottom row shows displacements of a Ta atom diffusing in Cu at 1200 K by the direct interstitial mechanism. In the latter case, the displacements do not form a string. The snapshots are shown at three sequential moments in time from left to right. The atoms are color-coded by the potential energy to show that the head atom of the string has an excess energy (red color). (b) A conceptual diagram illustrating the backward propagation of atomic displacement by the vacancy mechanism and forward propagation in the indirect and direct interstitial mechanisms. Some of the atoms are colored for easier tracking. \label{fig:lattice_1}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{Figure3} \par\end{centering} \caption{Examples of strings in disordered structures. (a) Backward and forward propagating strings in (a) $\Sigma29$ $(250)$ tilt grain boundary in Cu at 1000 K and (b) undercooled liquid Cu at 1000 K. In both cases, the lower row presents an example of direct forward propagation of a single atom that does not create a string. The snapshots are shown at three sequential moments in time from left to right. The atoms are color-coded by the potential energy. \label{fig:Disordered_1}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{Figure4} \par\end{centering} \caption{String nucleation at implanted densitons. (a) Top view of the $\Sigma29$ $(250)$ tilt grain boundary in Cu at 1000 K, showing its disordered structure. Atoms in face-centered cubic (FCC) environments are colored in yellow, while all other atoms are colored red. (b) Five atoms were randomly inserted into the boundary, creating high-density sites (positive densitons). After a 4 ps NVE MD run, all four densitons gave rise to forward-propagating strings. One of them is shown in the inset. (c) Five random atoms were removed from the boundary, creating low-density sites (negative densitons). After a 1.75 ps NVE MD run, four of them gave rise to backward-propagating strings. One of them is shown in the inset. One low-density site (encircled in the top right corner) delocalized without creating a string. \label{fig:Disordered_2}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{Figure5} \caption{(a) A log-log plot of the mean square displacement (MSD) versus time for copper at 1000 K, comparing a perfect lattice with a lattice containing one vacancy ($v$) or one interstitial atom ($i$). The respective defect concentrations (per lattice site) are $c_{v}=c_{i}=2.5\times10^{-4}$. The plot exhibits the ballistic, vibrational, and diffusion regimes of atomic motion. (b) Similar plot for Cu lattice with three different vacancy concentrations, with superimposed NGP plots for the respective concentrations. (c) Number of mobile atoms normalized by the number of vacancies versus the time interval $\Delta t$ for the three vacancy concentrations shown in (b). The inset is a zoom into the time interval with a relatively small number of mobile atoms per vacancy.\label{fig:lattice-MD}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{Figure6} \caption{Examples of strings and rings formed by sequential and concerted atomic rearrangements in Cu lattice and $\Sigma29$ $(250)$ tilt grain boundary (GB). The temperature is 1000\,K for all cases except for the concerted atomic rearrangement caused by an interstitial dumbbell in the Cu lattice at 500 K. The plots show displacements of individual atoms as a function of time interval $\Delta t$. The displacements are normalized by their maximum value during the period $\Delta t$. The curves have been smoothed using a running average to eliminate the noise due to vibrational motion. The schematic in the top right corner explains the definitions of the the transition/rearrangement time $\tau_{t}$ and the waiting time $\tau_{w}$.\label{fig:Strings-rings}}
elements.sent-2411-228: Mishin { \par \par\end{center \begin{center { { Department of Physics and Astronomy, MSN 3F3, George Mason University, Fairfax, Virginia 22030, USA { \par \par\end{center \begin{center {SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION { \par \par\end{center This file contains additional figures showing the investigations of string dynamics in systems other than those discussed in the main text.. {.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{FigureS1} \caption{String-like trajectories of atomic displacements in diamond-cubic silicon lattice at 1600 K. The model has the dimensions of $5.4\times5.4\times5.4$ nm$^{3}$. Periodic boundary conditions are applied along all spatial directions.}
elements.figure-2411-008: [H] \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{FigureS2} \caption{String-like trajectories of atomic displacements in amorphous silicon at 1000 K. The model has size of $10.9\times10.9\times10.9$ nm$^{3}$. Periodic boundary conditions are applied along all spatial directions. The amorphous state was prepared with the method proposed in \citep{Moon:2021aa}.}
elements.figure-2411-009: [H] \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{FigureS3} \caption{String-like trajectories of atomic displacements at the $\Sigma$85 (100) twist grain boundary in silicon at 1600 K. The model has the dimensions of $14\times14\times32.5$ nm$^{3}$. Periodic boundary conditions are applied along the directions parallel to the grain boundary plane.}
elements.figure-2411-010: [H] \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{FigureS4} \caption{String-like trajectories of atomic displacements at the Al(Si)(110)/Si(001) interphase boundary in the Al/Si system at 648 K. The model has the dimensions of $21.5\times21.5\times16$ nm$^{3}$. Periodic boundary conditions are applied along the directions parallel to the interface. The structure was constructed by depositing Al and Si on a Si(001) substrate at 648 K. }
elements.figure-2411-011: [H] \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{FigureS5} \caption{String-like trajectories of atomic displacements at the Cu(110)/Ta(110) interphase boundary at 1000 K. The model has the dimensions of $15.9\times12.6\times28.5$ nm$^{3}$. Periodic boundary conditions are applied along the directions parallel to the interface.}
elements.figure-2411-012: [H] \begin{centering} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{FigureS6} \par\end{centering} \caption{Closed string-like trajectories (rings) for the above-mentioned systems.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Yet another method identifies $\Delta t$ with the inflection point on the mean-square displacement (MSD) plot as a function of time, giving a similar value.
elements.sent-2411-230: Such trajectories carry information about the relaxation dynamics of the density fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-231: Furthermore, if two different trajectories accidentally cross, each of them will be chopped into two parts.
elements.sent-2411-232: In the subsequent MD simulations, most of the positive densitons drifted away from the initial location and created a forward-propagating string.
elements.sent-2411-233: Since point-defect jumps are dynamically memoryless, the jump activation time is the same as the average time increment of string growth, which we call the jump waiting time $\tau_{w}$.
elements.sent-2411-234: As expected, the vacancy-induced deviation of the MSD curve from the lattice behavior starts earlier as the vacancy concentration increases (Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice-MD (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-235: Returning to Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice-MD (b), note that the NGP maximum occurs on a significantly longer timescale than $\tau_{w}$.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Given the previously discussed similarity between the ordered and disordered system, the following conclusions can be made: (1) the time $t^{*}$ commonly used for string identification is too long ($\gg\tau_{w}$) and overlooks the key mechanisms of the string growth, and (2) $t^{*}$ reflects not only the growth of individual strings but also the degree of disorder in the system.
elements.sent-2411-237: We are aware of only two prior investigations of atomic displacements in individual strings \citep{10.1063/1.1644539,Erhart2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-238: In \citep{10.1063/1.1644539 , the displacements were found to propagate sequentially with occasional collective jumps of small atomic groups called ``micro-strings''.}
elements.sent-2411-239: However, the string was treated as a physically distinct dynamical object with a ``backbone'' hosting the micro-strings.
elements.sent-2411-240: Because the entire string emerged in the simulation during the time increment $\Delta t$, this gave the impression that the atoms had moved collectively.
elements.sent-2411-241: On the other hand, MD simulations capture purely computational string-truncation processes such as their mutual intersections and self-intersections.
elements.sent-2411-242: Nevertheless, the observation of strings is useful as a means of locating the densitons and establishing their sign.
elements.sent-2411-243: Instead of focusing on the strings, the atomic dynamics can be better described by a random walk of positive and negative densitons populating the system and exhibiting a spectrum of mobilities and lifetimes.
elements.sent-2411-244: The densiton migration can be responsible for the atomic self-diffusion and possibly other transport properties of disordered systems.
elements.sent-2411-245: The bicrystal had the approximate dimensions of $15\times15\times30$ nm$^{3}$.
elements.sent-2411-247: Acknowledgments We are grateful to Dr.~Ian Chesser for carefully reading the manuscripts and providing helpful suggestions.
elements.sent-2411-248: This research was supported by the U.S.
elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{Figure4} \par\end{centering} \caption{String nucleation at implanted densitons. (a) Top view of the $\Sigma29$ $(250)$ tilt grain boundary in Cu at 1000 K, showing its disordered structure. Atoms in face-centered cubic (FCC) environments are colored in yellow, while all other atoms are colored red. (b) Five atoms were randomly inserted into the boundary, creating high-density sites (positive densitons). After a 4 ps NVE MD run, all five densitons gave rise to forward-propagating strings. One of them is shown in the inset. (c) Five random atoms were removed from the boundary, creating low-density sites (negative densitons). After a 1.75 ps NVE MD run, four of them gave rise to backward-propagating strings. One of them is shown in the inset. One low-density site (encircled in the top right corner) delocalized without creating a string. \label{fig:Disordered_2}}
elements.sent-2411-249: Here, we show by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations that strings involving more than a few atoms do not form by a single collective event.
elements.sent-2411-250: Instead, they represent trajectories of propagating local density perturbations, which we call densitons.
elements.sent-2411-251: The atoms on this trajectory are almost indistinguishable from their environments except for the moving head of the string (densiton).
elements.sent-2411-252: Keywords: Diffusion, modeling, point defects, disordered systems.
elements.sent-2411-253: The atomic dynamics in fully or partially disordered materials controls many of their properties, such as diffusive mass transport, viscosity, and heat conduction \citep{Berthier2011,Derlet:2018aa,Derlet:2020aa,derlet2021viscosity,Derlet:2021aa,AlSm1 .}
elements.sent-2411-254: The MD simulations reported in this article reveal that strings containing more than 2-4 atoms do not form by a single coordinated/cooperative atomic displacement.
elements.sent-2411-255: Instead, such strings represent trajectories of density fluctuations propagating through the material by a series of discrete atomic rearrangements.
elements.sent-2411-256: However, the present simulations challenge the previously claimed association of the strings with collective displacements of large ($n\gg3$) atomic groups.
elements.sent-2411-257: Thus, the strings shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_1 (a) represent the atomic trajectories caused by the point defect migration.}
elements.sent-2411-258: The atoms forming the strings are almost indistinguishable from the surrounding atoms, except for the head atom, which possess excess energy due to its proximity to the defect.
elements.sent-2411-259: In other words, the physically distinct part of a string is its moving head.
elements.sent-2411-260: The rest of the string has had sufficient time to undergo nearly complete structural relaxation and is revealed in MD simulations due to the computer's ability to label and track individual atoms.
elements.sent-2411-261: While this term is redundant in the context of crystalline solids, it facilitates the subsequent discussion of strings in disordered systems, in which vacancies and interstitials are not well-defined while the respective density fluctuations play similar roles.\footnote{The term ``densiton'' was previously mentioned in the context of sound-like excitations in ionic liquids \citep{Apostol:2008aa .}}
elements.sent-2411-262: The densitons introduced here have a fundamentally different meaning.
elements.sent-2411-263: In a relatively small system, the maximum string length is on the order of the system size.
elements.sent-2411-264: To summarize the above analysis, the standard algorithm based on the $\Delta t=t^{*}$ time interval identifies relatively long strings comprising $n\gg3$ atoms.
elements.sent-2411-265: Such strings can rarely form by a single collective event.
elements.sent-2411-266: They represent a chain of dynamically uncorrelated atomic rearrangements typically involving from 1 to 3 atoms.
elements.sent-2411-267: The string represents the densiton's trajectory, which is physically almost indistinguishable from the background except for its head.
elements.sent-2411-268: Occasionally, a group of $n_{c =2$ to $4$ atoms can participate in the jump.}
elements.sent-2411-269: The latter process apparently represented a string nucleation, although its nature was not studied in detail.
elements.sent-2411-270: The time interval of this transition is approximately $10^{-1}\lesssim\Delta t\lesssim1$ ps, which is comparable to the inverse of the Debye frequency of Cu ($\nu_{D =7.14\times10^{12}$ Hz at 0 K estimated from $\nu_{D =k_{B T_{D /h$ using the Debye temperature of $T_{D =343$ K \citep{Kittel ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-271: For concerted atomic rearrangements involving several atoms, $\tau_{t}$ is somewhat longer but still below $\tau_{w}$.
elements.sent-2411-272: To quantify the timescales $\tau_{w}$ and $\tau_{t}$ more precisely and estimate the number $n_{c}$ of atoms involved in collective transitions, consider the disordered $\Sigma29$~\{250\} grain boundary in Cu as a representative case.
elements.sent-2411-273: This boundary already appeared in Figs.~\ref{fig:1 , \ref{fig:Disordered_1 and \ref{fig:Disordered_2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-274: Additional MD runs were performed for this boundary in the NVE ensemble at the temperature of 800 K.
elements.sent-2411-275: All string-like atomic rearrangements were detected by the method explained in section \ref{sec:Methods and stored in the computer memory.}
elements.sent-2411-276: Collective transitions manifested themselves in sudden jumps in displacement-time plots, as was shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Strings-rings .}
elements.sent-2411-277: Statistics were collected for the durations $\tau_{t}$ of such jumps.
elements.sent-2411-278: To estimate $\tau_{t}$, the jump event was fitted by a $\tanh(t/\tau_{t )$ function, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:Evaluation-of-tau (a,b).}}
elements.sent-2411-279: The statistical distribution of $\tau_{t}$ peaks between 1 and 2 ps.
elements.sent-2411-280: In the example shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Evaluation-of-tau (c), the peak is at $\tau_{t}\approx1.6$ ps.}
elements.sent-2411-281: Based on these statistics, we take $\tau_{t =2$ ps as a reasonable estimate of $\tau_{t}$ for this grain boundary.}
elements.sent-2411-282: To estimate the number of atoms $n_{c}$ participating in collective rearrangements, we scanned the history of a 100 ps long MD run with a moving time window of width $\Delta t$=$\tau_{t}$, counting the number of atoms undergoing string-like rearrangements within this window.
elements.sent-2411-283: By the meaning of $\tau_{t}$, such rearrangements are collective in nature.
elements.sent-2411-284: Fig.~\ref{fig:histograms shows the obtained statistical distributions of $n_{c}$ values for the temperatures of 600 K and 800 K.}
elements.sent-2411-285: At 600 K, 56\% of the collective events involve $n_{c =2$ atoms, another 38\% involve $n_{c =3$ atoms, and only 3.5\% of the collective events involve $n_{c =4$ atoms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-286: At 800 K, the respective percentages are 68\%, 23\%, and 6.7\%.
elements.sent-2411-287: The shape of the distribution changes with temperature, but the conclusion remains that the overwhelming majority of the collective rearrangements involve between 2 and 3 atoms.
elements.sent-2411-288: Fig.~\ref{fig:histograms additionally displays the histograms for much wider time windows $\Delta t$.}
elements.sent-2411-289: Note that the string size increases with $\Delta t$ because the wider time windows ($\Delta t\gg\tau_{t}$) capture longer strings formed by sequences of several collective events.
elements.sent-2411-290: As was illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:Strings-rings , the waiting time $\tau_{w}$ between the collective events is significantly longer than $\tau_{t}$.}
elements.sent-2411-291: This is further confirmed by the plot of the string lifetime versus the string size $n$ in Fig.~\ref{fig:lifetimes .}
elements.sent-2411-292: The string lifetime was extracted from the simulation history by detecting the time when the string first appeared and the time after which it no longer grew.
elements.sent-2411-293: The large scatter of the points in the long-time limit reflects the broader distribution of the lifetimes of longer strings.
elements.sent-2411-294: The plot shows that it takes hundreds of picoseconds to grow a string composed of 10 or more atoms.
elements.sent-2411-295: A 10-atom string could be formed by 3 to 5 back-to-back atomic rearrangements, which would only take 6 to 10 ps.
elements.sent-2411-296: The fact that it actually takes $\sim100$ ps to grow such a string confirms the intermittent nature of the string growth with long ($\tau_{w}\gg\tau_{t}$) time intervals separating the collective events.
elements.sent-2411-297: It should be noted that the above estimates of $n_{c}$ and the timescales $\tau_{w}$ and $\tau_{t}$ were obtained for a particular (although rather broad) set of disordered systems at relatively high temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-298: We did not consider other disordered systems, such as metallic glasses, at low temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-299: In such systems, the numerical values of the above parameters could be different, although the described mechanism of string growth is likely to remain qualitatively similar.
elements.sent-2411-300: The timescale of collective atomic rearrangements and the dephasing time after each collective event should still be in the picosecond range (roughly, several vibration periods).
elements.sent-2411-301: More atoms could be involved in the collective events than at high temperatures, but the difference is unlikely to be dramatic.
elements.sent-2411-302: The densiton lifetime can be longer, resulting in longer strings.
elements.sent-2411-303: But the strings should still grow intermittently with relatively long ($\gg1$ ps) periods between fast ($\sim1$ ps) collective rearrangements.
elements.sent-2411-304: These predictions require validation by direct MD simulations in the future.
elements.sent-2411-305: The statistical methods employed included the analysis of MSD-time functions \citep{Donati_1999,Berthier2011,Douglas98,Kob_97,AlSm1,10.1063/1.1644539 , the van Hove spatial correlation function \citep{10.1063/1.1644539,Berthier2011,Derlet:2021aa,AlSm1 , the self-intermediate scattering functions \citep{AlSm1,10.1063/1.1644539 , the size distributions of dynamics clusters \citep{Donati_1999,Douglas98,Kob_97,AlSm1,chandler2010dynamics,jung2005dynamical,10.1063/1.1644539,Karmakar:2009aa,Cui:2001aa , and other statistical measured demonstrating dynamic heterogeneity.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-306: Importantly, the part of the string left behind the moving head is structurally relaxed and physically almost indistinguishable from the surrounding atoms.
elements.sent-2411-307: It can still be revealed by tracking the atoms in MD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-308: The moving head of the string is physically the most distinct part due to its excess energy and (positive or negative) local free volume.
elements.sent-2411-309: The present simulations call for a significant reinterpretation of the strings and rings in disordered structures as trajectories of moving densitons and not dynamic objects formed by a single collective atomic rearrangement.

elements.figure-2411-014: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{Figure7} \par\end{centering} \caption{Evaluation of the collective transition timescale $\tau_{t}$ from MD simulations of the disordered $\Sigma29$~\{250\} grain boundary in Cu at 800 K. (a,b) Representative displacement-time plots for single-atom transitions and their fits with a $\tanh(t/\tau_{t})$ function (dashed lines) to extract the $\tau_{t}$ values. (a) Example of small $\tau_{t}$. (b) Example of large $\tau_{t}$. (c) Probability distribution of $\tau_{t}$ values.\label{fig:Evaluation-of-tau}}
elements.figure-2411-015: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[width=0.53\textwidth]{Figure8} \par\end{centering} \caption{Evaluation of the number of atoms $n_{c}$ participating in collective transitions. The disordered $\Sigma29$~\{250\} grain boundary in Cu at (a) 600 K and (b) 800 K is used as an example. The statistical distributions of the string size are shown for three values of the time window $\Delta t$ indicated in the key. The window $\Delta t=\tau_{t}$ detects predominantly collective atomic rearrangements.\label{fig:histograms}}
elements.figure-2411-016: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[width=0.55\textwidth]{Figure9} \par\end{centering} \caption{The string lifetime as a function of string length $n$ in the disordered $\Sigma29$~\{250\} grain boundary in Cu at 600 K. \label{fig:lifetimes}}

elements.figure-2411-017: [H] \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{FigureS4} \caption{String-like trajectories of atomic displacements at the Al(Si)(110)/Si(001) interphase boundary in the Al/Si system at 648 K. The model has the dimensions of $21.5\times21.5\times16$ nm$^{3}$. Periodic boundary conditions are applied along the directions parallel to the interface. The structure was constructed by depositing Al and Si on a Si(001) substrate at 648 K.}
